headline: CRUDE RUNS 6%, BEANS FIRM, CORN SPLITS AGAIN
subheadline: Trump's Iran blockade reinstatement drove WTI's biggest single-session gain in months, while the corn nearby/December gap quietly tells the real crop story.
lead: Crude ran 5.9% overnight to $78.66 after President Trump reinstated the Iran blockade, the biggest energy move in months and one that wasn't on any farmer's radar at yesterday's close. But the grain complex didn't follow energy's lead: soybeans firmed to $12.07 nearby while December corn slipped to $4.63, widening the nearby/December inversion to 22 cents. That split is the market asking whether the pollination stress Northeast Missouri farmers are crossing their fingers over is priced in yet or still coming.
the_takeaway: Crude's blockade move changes your diesel math; lock fall fuel before the next escalation leg.
teaser: Trump's Iran blockade reinstatement lit up crude; the corn market is still arguing about pollination.

one_number.unit: USDA beef export data revision, sales cut
one_number.context: USDA dramatically lowered its reported beef export sales figures, cutting reported volumes by roughly 90%, according to Reuters. That is not a rounding error; it is a data integrity problem that directly undercuts any bullish export thesis for the cattle complex. When the government's own numbers can't be trusted at that magnitude, producers relying on USDA export data to make marketing decisions are flying partly blind.

yesterdays_call.note: WTI closed at $78.66 today -- the Iran blockade reinstatement delivered the directional move, but crude stopped $1.34 short of the $80.00 target. The thesis was right; the level did not play out. With WTI now sitting just below $80 and the blockade still in force, the call is still live heading into Wednesday.

sections.title: Crude Blockade Move
sections.body: WTI crude ran 5.9% to **$78.66**, the overnight session's loudest move after President Trump reinstated the Iran blockade, forcing traders to reprice supply disruption risk they had spent the last several weeks unwinding. The Iran-Hormuz situation, which had been deflating on diplomatic progress through Swiss intermediaries since early April, just reset. Tanker traffic through the Strait remained navigable as recently as last week, but that discount is gone now. **Refiners are actually in a sweet spot**: crude is back up but diesel and gasoline never fully came off their highs, meaning crack spreads widened further today. For producers booking fall harvest diesel, this is the worst possible timing; the window from last week, when WTI was still soft, is closed.
sections.catalyst: Trump reinstates Iran blockade; oil prices ran more than 8% Monday per OilPrice.com.
sections.bottom_line: Lock fall diesel now. The next escalation leg has no ceiling yet.
sections.farmer_action: Producers without fall diesel hedged: today's $78.66 WTI is already above last week's scale-in window. Lock at least 50% of harvest-season diesel needs at current basis-adjusted prices. A second blockade development would move this market another 5-8% before the commercials blink.
sections.vs_yesterday: Crude: yesterday's giveback opportunity is gone; blockade reinstatement reset the premium.

sections.title: Beans Firm, Meal Slips
sections.body: Soybeans nearby firmed to **$12.07**, up nearly a dollar a bushel since last week, while November held at $11.94. The old-crop/new-crop carry is slight at 13 cents and that structure is not screaming urgency either direction. The real tension is inside the complex: **soybean oil ran 2.1% to $71.09** while meal fell 1.2% to $315.60, telling you the biofuel bid is driving this rally more than protein demand. Missouri farmers note soybeans look excellent heading into August, which is the real yield-setting window, and that good-crop setup is probably keeping November from matching nearby's enthusiasm. The China $17 billion annual purchase commitment, announced May 18, has not translated into a fresh sustained futures leg; Thursday's export sales will tell you whether that commitment is booking or sitting.
sections.catalyst: Soybean oil biofuel bid running; Missouri field reports show strong soybean condition heading into August.
sections.bottom_line: Old-crop beans are real money; new-crop needs Thursday's export sales to confirm the thesis.
sections.farmer_action: Producers with unpriced old-crop beans: $12.07 is at the top of the 52-week range at 94% from the low. Scale sales on 20-25% increments here. New crop November at $11.94: hold through Thursday's export sales. Above 400K MT confirms the bid; below 300K MT, the biofuel rally is carrying more weight than demand.
sections.vs_yesterday: Beans: old-crop/new-crop split held; oil/meal divergence widened further today.

sections.title: Corn: Pollination Pressure Builds
sections.body: Nearby corn eased to $4.41 while December slipped to **$4.63**, keeping the inversion at 22 cents. Yesterday's crop progress report is the thread this week runs on, and the question is whether Monday's condition data showed stress showing up in the ratings after dry pollination weather across parts of northeast Missouri and eastern Iowa. A farmer near West Liberty, Iowa says she has never experienced growing-season extremes like this year: really dry, really wet, really cold, really hot. **December needs to hold above $4.58** to maintain the weather premium thesis; today's close at $4.63 is still above that line, but the 1.0% decline tells you the funds are not adding conviction at these levels. The 2026 planting season, behind average through May before catching up between rain systems, gave the Belt a strong start, but pollination stress is the one thing the early-season calendar didn't price.
sections.catalyst: Missouri and Iowa producer reports of dry conditions during corn pollination; ongoing extreme weather variability across the Belt.
sections.bottom_line: December corn at $4.63 is still above the weather-premium line; Monday's crop progress is the week's scorecard.
sections.vs_yesterday: Corn: December held above $4.58 called level; split structure persists.

sections.title: Cattle Quiet, Data Problem
sections.body: Live cattle eased just two cents to **$234.95** and feeders gave back a dime to $354.20. The complex is effectively marking time, which would read as patience except for the 90% USDA beef export data revision that Reuters reported today. That is not a statistical footnote: USDA dramatically lowered its reported beef export sales, removing a key prop from any bullish export narrative. The ongoing Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler plant lockout, with 1,700 workers still out and roughly 6,000 head of daily processing capacity offline, continues to constrain the market from the supply side rather than the demand side. **The data revision plus the lockout equals a complex where the buy-side thesis just got murkier.** Mexican cattle producers are also pressing hard to reopen the U.S. border to live cattle trade, which would add supply-side weight if it gains traction.
sections.catalyst: USDA revised beef export sales down 90% per Reuters report; Cargill lockout ongoing with no resolution.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle patience looks thinner now that the export data prop just got pulled.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: live held, but USDA data revision undercuts the export bullish case.

sections.title: Milk Firms, Hogs Hold
sections.body: Class III milk firmed 2.8% to **$17.11**, recovering a portion of Monday's sharp drop. Yesterday's hog close at $98.15 held the $90 level comfortably, and today's 0.9% slip is a normal pullback after last week's 10.8% run. **Hogs at $98.15 are still historically elevated** and the funds that pushed that move are sitting on open profits; a further drift toward $94-95 would be the healthy consolidation pattern before any next leg. Milk's bounce is more interesting: Class III is at 76% of its 52-week range after the prior session's hard drop, and the cheese market will tell you whether this is a one-day correction or something with legs.
sections.catalyst: Class III partial recovery after Monday's sharp drop; hog pullback after prior session's outsized gain.
sections.bottom_line: Hogs consolidating near $98; milk bounce needs cheese market confirmation.

outside_the_pit.title: USDA Plans to Rescind Three Packers and Stockyards Rules
outside_the_pit.body: The USDA Unified Agenda lists rescission of three Packers and Stockyards Act proposed rules as a regulatory goal. For cattle producers already watching packer concentration through the Cargill lockout lens, fewer Packers and Stockyards guardrails means the structural leverage question does not get easier.

outside_the_pit.title: FTC Settlement Gives Farmers Right to Repair John Deere Equipment
outside_the_pit.body: A federal FTC settlement now requires John Deere to allow farmers and independent mechanics to access repair tools and software, ending decades of authorized-dealer-only restrictions. For producers facing harvest-season breakdowns, this is a real operational change, not just a policy win.

outside_the_pit.title: Oklahoma Poultry Pollution Suit Reaches $41.7M Settlement
outside_the_pit.body: Integrators must pay $41.7 million into Oklahoma's state environmental fund and help contract growers install riparian buffers following the long-running poultry litter pollution lawsuit. Contract growers in the watershed watch carefully: buffer installation requirements carry real land and compliance costs.

spread_to_watch.label: Soybeans nearby / November new-crop
spread_to_watch.commentary: Old-crop nearby soybeans at $12.07 versus November at $11.94 puts the carry at just 13 cents with harvest still three months out. That narrow carry is telling you the market is not worried about storage economics or old-crop tightness; it is telling you the biofuel and export bid is lifting nearby without conviction on whether new-crop demand justifies following. If Thursday's export sales come in above 400K MT, that 13-cent gap closes and November starts playing catch-up.

basis.headline: Corn basis softening; soybean basis firm in eastern Belt.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is softening as harvest-pressure expectations build ahead of the July pollination read. **Soybean basis in the eastern Belt is firm**, consistent with strong old-crop demand and tight nearby carry. Western Belt corn and bean basis both running seasonally soft. Wheat basis is tightening in the northern Plains as South Dakota harvest comes in better than expected with strong grain quality, which should push more volume through country elevators in the next 2-3 weeks.

weekly_thread.question: Will USDA Crop Progress confirm the good-crop thesis that nearby corn is already pricing, or does a condition decline put the weather premium back on the board?
weekly_thread.status_text: Monday's crop progress landed while field reports from northeast Missouri and eastern Iowa are flagging dry stress during pollination, exactly the conditions that would put the weather premium back in play. December corn closed $4.63 today, still above the $4.58 line, but the funds are not buying this level with conviction.

the_more_you_know.title: What Refiner Crack Spreads Say That Crude Prices Don't
the_more_you_know.body: Crude ran to $78.66 today on the Iran blockade reinstatement, but the energy story your input costs care about is the crack spread: the gap between what refiners pay for crude and what they charge for diesel and gasoline. Today's OilPrice.com reporting confirms refiners are in one of the best profit environments in years, because crude came off its highs over the past several weeks while diesel and gasoline stayed stubbornly expensive. That means when crude rips back up 6% in a session, you pay for both the higher crude and the unchanged refiner margin on top. The practical read: the last time WTI was at $78 before the Hormuz premium built, diesel was cheaper at the rack. Today it is not. If you are booking fall harvest fuel, the price you see is not a crude-equivalent price; it is a crude-plus-intact-margin price, and that margin does not compress until refinery capacity comes back online or demand softens.

watch_list.desc: USDA Weekly Export Sales: soybeans above 400K MT confirms the biofuel-plus-demand bid and opens November toward $12.10; below 300K MT, the old-crop rally is running on oil alone.
Iran blockade: if Trump's reinstatement holds without diplomatic response, WTI above $80 becomes the next test and diesel basis adjusts upward within 48-72 hours.
USDA Crop Progress: corn condition below 60% good/excellent reopens December toward $4.75; above 65% removes the pollination premium and December tests $4.55 support.
Cargill Fort Morgan/Schuyler lockout resolution: any settlement removes the 2% weekly slaughter constraint and live cattle face fresh selling pressure below $235.

USDA beef export data revision follow-through: a 90% downward revision raises the question of whether prior weekly export sales reports were overstated; watch for USDA clarification or methodology statement.

daily_quote.text: A bad penny always turns up.

source_summary: LOCKED PRICE TABLE (CME settlements July 14, 2026); Brownfield Ag News; OilPrice.com; Farm Policy News (Illinois); Reuters via Farm Policy News; The Fence Post; Feedstuffs; Beef Magazine; farmdoc daily; EIA.
